narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after pouring your heart into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we could support—it's not about you as a person, but the patterns it spotted in your profile that echoed too many past struggles. At the top of what stood out was your Year 3 decile of 1.0, the strongest signal pulling toward failure, as students in that bottom rung have historically faced steep climbs to pass the bar. Right behind it was your Year 1 decile of 1.0, reinforcing that early academic hurdles at school often snowball into bar challenges. Your racial background as Black weighed in third, reflecting tougher odds in the data for students like you navigating systemic barriers that show up in pass rates. Then there's your lower-middle family income, which the model linked to added pressures that have tripped up others in similar spots. Your LSAT score of 34, while solid for some, nudged things further toward risk in this mix, as did your undergraduate GPA of 2.4, painting a picture of inconsistent academic momentum. Even being full-time and male played small but consistent roles in the direction of caution, based on how those traits have correlated with outcomes before. Together, these factors tipped the scale to a 57% predicted chance of bar failure—not a sure thing, but enough in our careful review to pause on your application. You've got real fire targeting law school, and this isn't the end; maybe bolstering those early grades or seeking targeted support could rewrite your story next time. Hang in there—you're more than these numbers.
